title: Buffer Copy without Checking Size of Input
cert_c_title: Guarantee that storage for strings has sufficient space for character data and the null terminator
mitigation: Use bounded string manipulation functions (e.g., strncpy instead of strcpy). Ensure that target buffers have sufficient length to hold the maximum possible source size.

title: Use After Free
mitigation: Set pointers to NULL immediately after freeing them. Avoid using pointers that point to deallocated allocations.

title: NULL Pointer Dereference
mitigation: Check all returned pointers against NULL before dereferencing them. Defensive programming checks must encompass all memory allocations.

title: Use of Externally-Controlled Format String
cert_c_title: Exclude user input from format strings
mitigation: Do not pass user-controlled input as the format string argument. Always hardcode the format string directly.

title: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command
mitigation: Never use the system() command with unfiltered user strings. Use specialized API frameworks like exec() families that take strictly tokenized argument arrays.

title: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory
cert_c_title: Canonicalize path names originating from untrusted sources
mitigation: Ensure any user-provided path inputs do not contain parent directory (`../`) sequences and canonicalize them prior to evaluation.
vulnerable_example:
File f = new File("/var/www/" + filename);
f.read();
secure_example:
Path path = Paths.get("/var/www/", filename).normalize();
if(!path.startsWith("/var/www/")) throw new SecurityException();

title: Improper Input Validation
cert_c_title: Sanitize data passed to complex subsystems
mitigation: Validate inputs against expected structural bindings and typecast them securely.

title: Improper Neutralization of Directives in Dynamically Evaluated Code
mitigation: Do not pass user data as evaluation functions (`eval()`, `exec()`). Use ast.literal_eval() for complex strings instead.

title: Deserialization of Untrusted Data
mitigation: Halt usage of native language serializers (e.g., Python `pickle` or Java serialization). Use JSON or strictly verified parser architectures.

title: Use of Hard-coded Credentials
mitigation: Do not hardcode API keys, passwords, or connection strings into repositories. Mount them through deployment environment variables.

title: Missing Release of Memory after Effective Lifetime
cert_c_title: Free dynamically allocated memory when no longer needed
mitigation: Ensure allocated blocks have explicit de-allocator mapping calls tracking all error/exit branches.

title: Integer Overflow or Wraparound
cert_c_title: Ensure that unsigned integer operations do not wrap
mitigation: Check that scalar limits strictly do not wrap integers during loops mapping buffers.
secure_example:
if (size > MAX_INT / 4) abort();
int total = size * 4;

title: Incorrect Permission Assignment for Critical Resource
cert_c_title: Create files with appropriate access permissions
mitigation: Define strict umasks and restrict file creation payloads locking read privileges from World or Guest processes.

title: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injection')
mitigation: Remove string concatenation around SQL statement commands. Shift to strict PrepareStatement parameters securely mapping bindings.
vulnerable_example:
String query = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '" + username + "'";
stmt.executeQuery(query);

title: Improper Restriction of XML External Entity Reference
mitigation: Halt entity parsing and turn off external DTDs across your backend XML handlers.
vulnerable_example:
DocumentBuilderFactory dbf = DocumentBuilderFactory.newInstance();
Document doc = dbf.newDocumentBuilder().parse(input);

title: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm
cert_c_title: Do not use insecure or weak cryptographic algorithms
mitigation: Stop mapping hashing systems like MD5/SHA1 over passwords. Transition to rigorous Key Derivation Functions (e.g. Bcrypt/Argon2).
